3 Stratified Sampling 3.1 What is Stratified Sampling? 3.2 Estimation 3.3 Design

3 Stratified Sampling 3.1 What is Stratified Sampling? 3.1.1 Stratify Sampling Steps Stratified sampling is composed of two steps: Stratification: Divide the population into \(H\) subpopulations, called strata, Each subpopulation is called a stratum The variable used to divide population is called the stratification variable. It is required that the strata: (i) do not overlap and (ii) constitute the whole population, such that each sampling unit belongs to exactly one stratum9. Sampling: Draw an independent probability sample from each stratum. Recall from Section 2.4 that a probability sample is when we know the sampling or inclusion probability for each unit in the population. If we draw a non-probability sample in each stratum, that is called quota sampling instead of startified sampling. In this chapter, we consider taking independent Simple Random Sampling (SRS) sample from each stratum. Check Section 2.6 for a refresh on SRS. Figure 3.1 illustrates stratified sampling, where the population is divided into strata of different colors, and from each of the stratum, some units are selected randomly and added into the sample. Figure 3.1: Visual illustration of stratified sampling technique. Example 3.1 In the students’ rent Example 1.1, we can divide the students at USask by their college affiliation such that each student is only affiliated to one college. Stratified sampling is when we independently take an SRS from each of the colleges. Now, college affiliation is the stratification variable. 3.1.2 Why Stratification? Stratification is usually used because It is more convenient to administer the survey and may lower costs Imagine having to get an email list of all students at the university, versus asking each college their student email list. Sometimes we want inference at specified confidence for subgroups Instead of only wanting to know the mean rent payment of all students at the university, we may also want to estimate the mean rent payment of students from each college precisely. Improve representativeness of the sample If we take an SRS from the whole university, we may end up with a sample without any student from Arts and Science. However, with stratified sampling, our sample will contain students from all colleges at USask. As we will see in later sections, stratified sampling usually gives more precise (i.e., lower variance) estimates for population means and totals. 3.2 Estimation 3.2.1 Population Parameters Suppose we divide the population \(U\) of \(N\) units into \(H\) strata: \(U_1, U_2, ..., U_H\). Note that, by definition in Section 3.1.1: These strata do not overlap, i.e., \(U_h \cap U_l = \emptyset\) for \(1 \le h 
e l \le H\). These strata comprise the full population, i.e., \(U = U_1 \cup U_2 \cup ... \cup U_H\). Suppose the \(h\)th stratum \(U_h\) contains \(N_h\) units, then \(\sum_{h=1}^H N_h = N\). Let \(y_{h,i}\) denote the response of the \(i\)th unit in the \(h\)th stratum. We then have the population and subpopulation parameters as follows: Stratum (subpopulation) \(h\) Population Total \(\color{red}{t_{U,h} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_h}y_{h,i}}\) \(\color{red}{t_U = \sum_{h=1}^H t_{U,h}} = \sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}y_{h,i}\) Mean \(\color{red}{\overline{y}_{U,h} =  rac{1}{N_h}t_{U,h}} =  rac{1}{N_h} \sum_{i=1}^{N_h}y_{h,i}\) \(\color{red}{\overline{y}_U =  rac{1}{N}t_U} =  rac{1}{N}\sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}y_{h,i}\) Variance \(\color{red}{s_{U,h}^2} =  rac{1}{N_h-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}(y_{h,i}-\overline{y}_{U,h})^2\) \(\color{red}{s_U^2} =  rac{1}{N-1}\sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}(y_{h,i}-\overline{y}_U)^2\) Example 3.2 Suppose we have the following information Stratum (\(h\)) Response from 1st unit (\(y_{h1}\)) \(y_{h2}\) \(y_{h3}\) \(y_{h4}\) \(y_{h5}\) 1 \(3\) \(5\) \(10\) \(1\) \(6\) 2 \(8\) \(10\) \(9\) So the first stratum has \(N_1 = 5\) units, the stratum total for the first stratum \(t_{U,1}\) is: \[ t_{U,1} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_1}y_{1i} = 3 + 5 + 10 + 1 + 6 = 25 \] The stratum mean is \[ \overline{y}_{U,1} =  rac{1}{N_1}t_{U,1} =  rac{25}{5} = 5. \] The second stratum has \(N_2 = 3\) units. The stratum total for the second stratum \(t_{U,2}\) is: \[ t_{U,2} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_2}y_{2i} = 8 + 10 + 9 = 27 \] The stratum mean is \[ \overline{y}_{U,2} =  rac{1}{N_2}t_{U,2} =  rac{27}{3} = 9. \] The overall total is \[ t_U = t_{U,1} + t_{U,2} = 25 + 27 = 3 + 5 + ... + 10 + 9 = 52. \] The overall mean is \[ \overline{y}_U =  rac{1}{N}t_U =  rac{1}{N_1 + N_2}t_U =  rac{52}{5+3} = 6.5 \] Notes: We cannot average the subpopulation means to get the overall population mean, i.e., \(\overline{y}_U 
e  rac{1}{H}\overline{y}_{U,h}\). This is because for each \(h\), the stratum mean \(\overline{y}_{U,h}\) represents \(N_h\) units of stratum \(h\) in the population. Therefore, we have to use a weighted average instead, with each \(\overline{y}_{U,h}\) weighted by the fraction of number of units in stratum \(h\) out of the whole population, i.e., \(N_h/N\). \[ \overline{y}_U =  rac{1}{N}\sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}y_{h,i}=\sum_{h=1}^H rac{N_h}{N}\overline{y}_{U,h} \] Example 3.3 Continue with Example 3.2, we see that \[ \overline{y}_U = 6.5 
e  rac{\overline{y}_{U,1} + \overline{y}_{U,2}}{2} =  rac{5+9}{2} = 7. \] Instead, we need to calculate \[ \overline{y}_U = \sum_{h=1}^H  rac{N_h}{N} \overline{y}_{U,h} =  rac{N_1}{N}\overline{y}_{U,1} +  rac{N_2}{N}\overline{y}_{U,2} =  rac{5}{5+3}	imes 5 +  rac{3}{5+3}	imes 9 = 6.5. \] Example 3.4 Suppose we have the following information: Stratum (\(h\)) Stratum size (\(N_h\)) Stratum mean (\(\overline{y}_{U,h}\)) Stratum total (\(t_{U,h} = N_h 	imes \overline{y}_{U,h}\)) 1 \(10\) \(3\) \(30\) 2 \(5\) \(7\) \(35\) 3 \(12\) \(6\) \(36\) The overall total is \(t_U = \sum_{h=1}^Ht_{U,h} = 30 + 35 + 36 = 101\). The overall mean is \(\overline{y}_U =  rac{1}{N}t_U =  rac{101}{10+5+12} = 3.7407\). Another method to calculate the overall mean is to take the weighted average of the stratum means: \[ \overline{y}_U = \sum_{h=1}^H rac{N_h}{N}\overline{y}_U =  rac{10}{10+5+12}	imes 3 +  rac{5}{10+5+12}	imes 7 +  rac{12}{10+5+12}	imes 6 = 3.7407. \] 3.2.2 Sample and HT Estimators As defined in Section 3.1.1, for each \(h\), we take an SRS sample \(S_h\) of \(n_h\) units from stratum \(U_h\). Then our overall sample consists of observation units from all these subsets: \(S = S_1 \cup S_2 \cup ... \cup S_H\) and the overall sample size is \(n = \sum_{h=1}^H n_h\). Recall from Section 2.3.3, the general formula of the Horvitz-Thompson estimator is \[ \widehat{T}_{HT} = \sum_{i \in S} rac{y_i}{\P(Z_i = 1)} = \sum_{i=1}^N  rac{Z_iy_i}{\pi_i}, \] where \(\pi_i\) is the probability that the \(i\)th observation unit is included in the sample. In the case of stratified sampling, we switch the enumeration: instead of enumerating (i.e., listing) all observation units all at once, we will first list out the strata \(h = 1, 2, ..., H\), then list the observation units within each stratum \(i = 1, 2, ..., n_h\). The formula becomes \[ egin{equation} \widehat{T}_{HT} = \sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i\in U_h} rac{y_{hi}}{\pi_{hi}} 	ag{3.1} \end{equation}\] To get the specific formula for the HT estimator, we need to find \(\pi_{hi}\), the probability that the \(i\)th unit of the \(h\)th stratum is included in the sample. Since for each stratum \(h\), we take an SRS of \(n_h\) observation units out of a subpopulation of \(N_h\) units, the selection probability is \(\pi_{hi} =  rac{n_h}{N_h}\). Therefore, the HT estimator for the overall total is \[ egin{equation} \widehat{T}_{HT} = \sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i\in U_h} rac{N_h}{n_h}y_{hi} = \sum_{h=1}^H \widehat{T}_{HT,h} 	ag{3.2} \end{equation}\] So the HT estimator for the overall total equals the sum of the HT estimators for the stratum totals. We can show that the HT estimator for the overall mean is the weighted average of the HT estimators for the stratum means: \[ egin{equation} \widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT} =  rac{1}{N}\widehat{T}_{HT} =  rac{1}{N}\sum_{h=1}^H\widehat{T}_{HT,h} =  rac{1}{N}\sum_{h=1}^HN_h \overline{Y}_{S,h} = \sum_{h=1}^H rac{N_h}{N} \widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT,h} 	ag{3.3} \end{equation}\] where each stratum \(h\) is weighted by \( rac{N_h}{N}\). Note here that we take SRS sample in each stratum, so HT estimates for stratum means are the sample stratum means, i.e., \(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT,h} = \overline{Y}_{S,h}\). In other words, we can use HT estimators to estimate the subpopulation total and mean for each stratum, then aggregate them together to estimate the overall total and mean. The estimators are summarized in the table below: HT estimators for stratum \(h\) HT estimators for whole population Mean \(\color{red}{\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT,h} = \overline{Y}_{S,h}} =  rac{1}{n_h} \sum_{i \in S_h}y_{h,i}\) \(\color{red}{\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT} =  rac{1}{N}\widehat{T}_{HT}} = \sum_{h=1}^H rac{N_h}{N}\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT,h}\) Total \(\color{red}{\widehat{T}_{HT,h} = N_h\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT,h}} =  rac{N_h}{n_h} \sum_{i \in S_h}y_{h,i}\) \(\color{red}{\widehat{T}_{HT} = \sum_{h=1}^H \widehat{T}_{HT,h}} = \sum_{h=1}^H N_h\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT,h}\) Notes: Here, the HT estimator for the overall population mean is not equal to the overall sample mean, i.e., \(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT} 
e \overline{Y}_S\). This is the result for SRS only. Here, we are dealing with stratified sampling, not SRS. In fact: \[ \widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT} = \sum_{h=1}^H rac{N_h}{N}\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT,h} = \sum_{h=1}^H rac{N_h}{N}\overline{Y}_{S,h} \] while \[ \overline{Y}_{S} = rac{1}{n}\sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i\in S_h}y_{ij} = \sum_{h=1}^H rac{n_h}{n}\overline{Y}_{S,h} \] 3.2.3 Variances In stratified sampling, SRS samples are taken independently from each stratum. Therefore, we can derive that \[ egin{equation} Var_{strt}\left(\widehat{T}_{HT}ight) = \sum_{h=1}^H Var_{SRS}(\widehat{T}_{HT,h}) = \sum_{h=1}^H N_h^2\left(1- rac{n_h}{N_h}ight) rac{s^2_{U,h}}{n_h}. 	ag{3.4} \end{equation}\] Proof. Recall in Section 2.7.2, we derived the variance of a general HT estimator as \[ Var(\widehat{T}_{HT}) = \sum_{i=1}^N rac{y_i^2}{\pi_i^2}Var(Z_i) + 2\sum_{i
e j} rac{y_iy_j}{\pi_i\pi_j}Cov(Z_i, Z_j) \] We can apply this to stratified sampling and derive \[ egin{align*} Var_{strt}(\widehat{T}_{HT}) & = \sum_{i=1}^N rac{y_i^2}{\pi_i^2}Var(Z_i) + 2\sum_{i
e j} rac{y_iy_j}{\pi_i\pi_j}Cov(Z_i, Z_j) \ & = \sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i=1}^{N_h} rac{y_{h,i}^2}{\pi_{h,i}^2}Var(Z_{h,i}) + 2\sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i
e j, i \in U_h, j \in U_h} rac{y_{h,i}y_{h,j}}{\pi_{h,i}\pi_{h,j}}Cov(Z_{h,i}, Z_{h,j}) \ & \qquad + 2\sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i \in U_h, j \in U_l, h
e l} rac{y_{h,i}y_{l,j}}{\pi_{h,i}\pi_{l,j}}Cov(Z_{h,i}, Z_{l,j}) \ & = \sum_{h=1}^H\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N_h} rac{y_{h,i}^2}{\pi_{h,i}^2}Var(Z_{h,i}) + 2\sum_{i
e j, i \in U_h, j \in U_h} rac{y_{h,i}y_{h,j}}{\pi_{h,i}\pi_{h,j}}Cov(Z_{h,i}, Z_{h,j})ight) \ & = \sum_{h=1}^H Var_{SRS}(\widehat{T}_{HT,h}) \end{align*}\] In the second equation, I first divide the population into subpopulations \(U_h\), \(h=1, 2, ..., H\). I then divide all pairs \(i,j\) into two categories: (i) units \(i\) and \(j\) are in the same stratum or (ii) they are in different strata. In the third equation, since SRS are taken independently from each stratum, if units \(i\) and \(j\) are from different strata, then \(Cov(Z_{h,i}, Z_{l,j}) = 0\). Finally, the last equation happens because SRS samples are taken from each stratum. Similarly, since \(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT} =  rac{1}{N}\widehat{T}_{HT}\), we have: \[ egin{equation} Var_{strt}\left(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT}ight) =  rac{1}{N^2}Var\left(\widehat{T}_{HT}ight) = \sum_{h=1}^H  rac{N_h^2}{N^2} \left(1- rac{n_h}{N_h}ight) rac{s^2_{U,h}}{n_h}. 	ag{3.5} \end{equation}\] 3.2.4 Variance Estimators From Equations (3.4) and (3.5) and the fact that each subpopulation are sampled using SRS, we can use the SRS variance estimators for each subpopulation and sum them together to get the variance estimators for the population, that is: \[ egin{equation} \widehat{Var}_{strt}\left(\widehat{T}_{HT}ight) = \sum_{h=1}^H \widehat{Var}_{SRS}(\widehat{T}_{HT,h}) = \sum_{h=1}^H N_h^2\left(1- rac{n_h}{N_h}ight) rac{S^2_{S,h}}{n_h}, 	ag{3.6} \end{equation}\] and \[ egin{equation} \widehat{Var}_{strt}\left(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT}ight) =  rac{1}{N^2} \widehat{Var}_{strt}\left(\widehat{T}_{HT}ight) = \sum_{h=1}^H  rac{N_h^2}{N^2} \left(1- rac{n_h}{N_h}ight) rac{S^2_{S,h}}{n_h}. 	ag{3.7} \end{equation}\] In other words, in Equations (3.4) and (3.5), we replace the subpopulation variances \(s^2_{U,h}\) (population quantities) by the sub-sample variances \(S^2_{S,h}\) (sample quantities). Example 3.5 Suppose we conducted stratified sampling and collected the following information: Stratum \(N_h\) \(n_h\) \(\overline{Y}_{S,h}\) \(S_{S,h}^2\) \(\widehat{T}_{HT,h}\) \(\widehat{Var}_{SRS}(\widehat{T}_{HT,h})\) \(A\) \(100\) \(20\) \(7\) \(2\) \(100 	imes 7 = 700\) \(100^2	imes \left(1- rac{20}{100}ight)	imes  rac{2}{20} = 800\) \(B\) \(200\) \(40\) \(4\) \(4\) \(200 	imes 4 = 800\) \(200^2	imes \left(1- rac{40}{200}ight)	imes  rac{4}{40} = 3200\) \(C\) \(300\) \(50\) \(6\) \(3\) \(300 	imes 6 = 1800\) \(300^2	imes \left(1- rac{50}{300}ight)	imes  rac{3}{50} = 4500\) To estimate the overall population total, we have \(\widehat{T}_{HT} = \widehat{T}_{HT, A} + \widehat{T}_{HT, B} + \widehat{T}_{HT, C} = 700 + 800 + 1800 = 3300\) \(\widehat{Var}(\widehat{T}_{HT}) = \widehat{Var}(\widehat{T}_{HT, A}) + \widehat{Var}(\widehat{T}_{HT, B}) + \widehat{Var}(\widehat{T}_{HT, C}) = 8500\) 95% CI: \(3300 \pm 1.96 	imes \sqrt{8500} = (3119.297, 3480.703)\) To estimate the overall population mean, we have \(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT} =  rac{1}{N}\widehat{T}_{HT} =  rac{3300}{600} = 5.5\). \(\widehat{Var}(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT}) =  rac{1}{N^2}\widehat{Var}(\widehat{T}_{HT}) =  rac{8500}{600^2} = 0.0236\) 95% CI: \(5.5 \pm 1.96 	imes \sqrt{0.0236} = (5.1989, 5.8011)\) 3.2.5 R Code We can use the survey package in R to calculate the estimate, standard errors and confidence intervals for stratified sampling. Suppose the population dataset is the fake rent dataset in Appendix A. (This is an artificially created example. In reality, you do not have ppopulation data). We load the dataset using the following code: #load the dataset dat <- read.csv("fakeRentPop.csv", header = TRUE) Now suppose we use stratified sampling to obtain a sample data samp_dat from the population data dat. To do this, we run the following code: #row indexes for each stratum idx_grad <- which(dat$study == "Grad") idx_med <- which(dat$study == "Med Residents") idx_non <- which(dat$study == "Non-degree") idx_under <- which(dat$study == "Undergrad") #take SRS in each stratum by sampling the row index in each stratum idx_samp_grad <- sample(idx_grad, size = 30, replace = FALSE) idx_samp_med <- sample(idx_med, size = 30, replace = FALSE) idx_samp_non <- sample(idx_non, size = 30, replace = FALSE) idx_samp_under <- sample(idx_under, size = 30, replace = FALSE) #obtain final sample dataset by combining the sampled row indexes samp_dat <- dat[c(idx_samp_grad, idx_samp_med, idx_samp_non, idx_samp_under),] Now, we can use survey package to analyze the sampled data set library(survey) #define N_h for each stratum Nhs <- tapply(dat$study, dat$study, length) #define an fpc dataset of two column #one for stratum size and the other is the corresponding level of study fpc <- data.frame(fpc = Nhs, study = names(Nhs)) #merge fpc dataset into the current dataset samp_dat <- merge(samp_dat, fpc, by = "study") #specify the stratified design for the sampled dataset design.strt <- svydesign(ids = ~1, strata = ~study, fpc = ~fpc, data = samp_dat) #estimating population mean svymean(~rent, design.strt) ## mean SE ## rent 788.32 52.814 #getting confidence interval confint(svymean(~rent, design.strt)) ## 2.5 % 97.5 % ## rent 684.8065 891.8318 3.3 Design 3.3.1 Approaches In Chapter 2, we considered two sampling schemes, Poisson sampling and SRS. To design a Poisson sampling scheme, we only need to specify only one design feature, which is the expected sample size \(n^*\). Similarly, for SRS, we only need to specify the sample size \(n\). In stratified sampling, we need to consider two design features, i.e., we need to: define the stratification variable, and determine the sample size \(n_h\) in each stratum \(h = 1,2, ..., H\). There are two approaches to the design of stratified sampling. First approach: Suppose we want precise estimates of \(\widehat{T}_{HT,h}\) or \(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT,h}\) for each stratum \(h\) at pre-specified level of confidence. In this case, the stratification variable is already well-defined from our research interest we only need to determine a tolerable margin of error \(e_h\) for each stratum \(h\), then proceed to separately calculate sample size \(n_h\) for each stratum \(h\) just like in SRS (see Section 2.9). Second approach: For a fixed general sample size \(n\), determine an allocation rule for \(n_h\) such that \[ n = n_1 + n_2 + ... + n_H. \] Based on the allocation rule, we Derive the formula of \(Var_{strt}(\widehat{T}_{HT})\) or \(Var_{strt}(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT})\) so that the formula is related to \(n\). Solve for \(n\) based on the formula and the tolerable margin of error \(e\). Determine \(n_h\) for each \(h = 1, 2, ..., H\) from the allocation rule. We will explore more about the second approach in the following sections. 3.3.2 Proportional Allocation 3.3.2.1 Definition and Properties Proportional allocation is when we sample proportional to the size of the stratum, that is \[ egin{equation} n_h \propto N_h. 	ag{3.8} \end{equation}\] This means that there exists a constant \(k\), which is not dependent on \(h\), such that \(n_h = kN_h\) for all \(h = 1, 2, ..., H\). However, we also know that \(\sum_{h=1}^Hn_h = n\). This implies \[ egin{equation}  rac{n}{N} =  rac{n_1}{N_1} =  rac{n_2}{N_2} = ... =  rac{n_H}{N_H}. 	ag{3.9} \end{equation}\] Proof. We have \[ egin{align*} n & = \sum_{h=1}^Hn_h \ & = \sum_{h=1}^H kN_h \ & = k \sum_{h=1}^H N_h \ & = k N \end{align*}\] Therefore, \(k =  rac{n}{N}\) and thus \(n_h =  rac{n}{N}N_h\) and \( rac{n_h}{N_h} =  rac{n}{N}\) for all \(h = 1, 2, ..., H\). With proportional allocation, the sample is a miniature version of the population, and thus, the sample is representative of the population in terms of the stratification variable. An illustration of proportional allocation is given in Figure 3.2, where we take a sample of \( rac{1}{3}\) the size of the population. The sample comprises of sub-samples that are \( rac{1}{3}\) the size of each stratum. The resulting sample is representative of the population in terms of color: one half of the sample is orange, one fourth is blue and the other one fourth is green. Figure 3.2: Visual illustration of proportional allocation. Example 3.6 Consider sampling from a population of \(N_1=2400\) men and \(N_2=1600\) women. If we take a \(10\%\) stratified sample with proportional allocation, our sample will have \(n_1 = N_1 	imes 10\% = 240\) men and \(n_2 = N_2 	imes 10\% = 160\) women. The sample now has the same proportions of men and women as the population. Another property of stratified sampling with proportional allocation is that it is self-weighting: the probability that the \(i\)th unit from stratum \(h\) is included in our sample is \(\pi_{h,i} =  rac{n_h}{N_h} =  rac{n}{N}\), since we do SRS within each stratum. Therefore, the weight for the \(i\)th unit from stratum \(h\) is \(w_{h,i} =  rac{1}{\pi_{h,i}} =  rac{N}{n}\). This weight does not depend on \(i\) and \(h\) and so it is the same across all units and strata. We also have that \[ \widehat{T}_{HT} = \sum_{h=1}^H \widehat{T}_{HT,h} = \sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i\in S_h} rac{N_h}{n_h}y_{h,i} = \sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i\in S_h} rac{N}{n}y_{h,i} \] In other words, each unit in the sample \(S = S_1 \cup ...\cup S_h\) represents the same number of units, i. \( rac{N}{n}\), in the population. Example 3.7 Continuing with Example 3.6, each unit in our sample will represent \( rac{1}{10\%}= 10\) people in the population. 3.3.2.2 Variances Plugging \( rac{n_h}{N_h} =  rac{n}{N}\) into Equation (3.4), we get \[ egin{equation} Var_{strt,prop}\left(\widehat{T}_{HT}ight) =  rac{N}{n}\left(1- rac{n}{N}ight)\sum_{h=1}^HN_hs_{U,h}^2. 	ag{3.10} \end{equation}\] Proof. We have \[ egin{align*} Var_{strt,prop}(\widehat{T}_{HT}) & = \sum_{h=1}^H Var_{SRS}(\widehat{T}_{HT,h}) \ & = \sum_{h=1}^H N_h^2\left(1- rac{n_h}{N_h}ight) rac{s_{U,h}^2}{n_h} \ & = \sum_{h=1}^H N_h  rac{N_h}{n_h}\left(1- rac{n_h}{N_h}ight)s_{U,h}^2 \ & =  rac{N}{n}\left(1- rac{n}{N}ight)\sum_{h=1}^HN_hs_{U,h}^2. \end{align*}\] Similarly, \[ egin{equation} Var_{strt,prop}\left(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT}ight) =  rac{1}{N^2}Var_{strt,prop}(\widehat{T}_{HT}) =  rac{1}{nN}\left(1- rac{n}{N}ight)\sum_{h=1}^HN_hs_{U,h}^2. 	ag{3.11} \end{equation}\] 3.3.2.3 Comparing to SRS In this section, we explore whether stratification is worthwhile, or whether we have better use SRS, in terms of estimating the population total (or mean). Note that in both cases, we are using the Horvitz-Thompson estimator, so we are unbiased. We only need to compare the variance then. Recall from Equation (2.14) that the variance of the Horvitz-Thompson estimator for the population total under SRS is \[ egin{equation} Var_{SRS}\left(\widehat{T}_{HT}ight) = N^2\left(1- rac{n}{N}ight) rac{s_U^2}{n} =  rac{N}{n}\left(1- rac{n}{N}ight)Ns_U^2 	ag{3.12} \end{equation}\] On the other hand, from Equation (3.10), the variance of the Horvitz-Thompson estimator for the population total under stratified sampling with proportional allocation is: \[ egin{equation} Var_{strt,prop}\left(\widehat{T}_{HT}ight) =  rac{N}{n}\left(1- rac{n}{N}ight)\sum_{h=1}^HN_hs_{U,h}^2. 	ag{3.13} \end{equation}\] Therefore, to compare \(Var_{SRS}\left(\widehat{T}_{HT}ight)\) and \(Var_{strt,prop}\left(\widehat{T}_{HT}ight)\), we need to compare \(Ns_U^2\) and \(\sum_{h=1}^HN_hs_{U,h}^2\). To make this comparison easier, we define Sum of Squares Notation Formula Meaning Between \(SSB\) \(\sum_{h=1}^HN_h(\overline{y}_{U,h} - \overline{y}_U)^2\) Between-strata variation Within \(SSW\) \(\sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}(y_{h,i}-\overline{y}_{U,h})^2\) Within-stratum variation Total \(SST\) \(\sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}(y_{h,i}-\overline{y}_U)^2\) Total variation The within-stratum variation measures the differences of units to its respective stratum means, the between-strata variation measures the differences of the stratum means to the overall means, and the total variation measures the differences of units to the overall means. Example 3.8 For example, the within-stratum variation sums over the differences such as of an Arts and Science student’s monthly rent payment to the Arts and Science mean, the between-stratum variation sums over the differences such as of the Arts and Science mean to the USask mean, and the total variation sums over the differences such as of a student to the USask mean. We can prove that \(SST = SSW + SSB\). Proof. We have \[ egin{align*} & SST \ & = \sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}(y_{h,i}-\overline{y}_U)^2 \ & = \sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}\left([y_{h,i}- \overline{y}_{U,h}] - [\overline{y}_U - \overline{y}_{U,h}] ight)^2 \ & = \sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}(y_{h,i}- \overline{y}_{U,h})^2 + \sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}(\overline{y}_U - \overline{y}_{U,h})^2 - 2\sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}(y_{h,i}- \overline{y}_{U,h})(\overline{y}_U - \overline{y}_{U,h}) \ & = \sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}(y_{h,i}- \overline{y}_{U,h})^2 + \sum_{h=1}^HN_h(\overline{y}_U - \overline{y}_{U,h})^2 - 2\sum_{h=1}^H(\overline{y}_U - \overline{y}_{U,h})\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}(y_{h,i}- \overline{y}_{U,h}) \ & = \sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}(y_{h,i}- \overline{y}_{U,h})^2 + \sum_{h=1}^HN_h(\overline{y}_{U,h} - \overline{y}_U)^2 - 2\sum_{h=1}^H(\overline{y}_U - \overline{y}_{U,h})\left[\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}y_{h,i}ight) - \left(N_h\overline{y}_{U,h}ight) ight] \ & = SSW + SSB - 2\sum_{h=1}^H(\overline{y}_U - \overline{y}_{U,h})\left[t_{U,h} - t_{U,h} ight] \ & = SSW + SSB - 0 \ & = SSW + SSB \end{align*}\] Using the definitions of \(SST, SSB\) and \(SSW\), we have \[ Ns_U^2 =  rac{N}{N-1}\sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}(y_{h,i}-\overline{y}_U)^2 =  rac{N}{N-1}SST =  rac{N}{N-1}(SSW + SSB) \] and \[ egin{align*} \sum_{h=1}^H N_hs_{U,h}^2 & = \sum_{h=1}^H rac{N_h}{N_h-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}(y_{h,i}-\overline{y}_{U,h})^2 = \sum_{h=1}^H\left(1+ rac{1}{N_h-1}ight)\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}(y_{h,i}-\overline{y}_{U,h})^2 \ & = SSW + \sum_{h=1}^H s_{U,h}^2 \end{align*}\] Plugging these into the formula of (3.12) and (3.13) we have \[ egin{equation} Var_{SRS}\left(\widehat{T}_{HT}ight) - Var_{strt,prop}\left(\widehat{T}_{HT}ight) =  rac{N}{n}\left(1- rac{n}{N}ight)  rac{N}{N-1} \left[ SSB - \sum_{h=1}^H \left(1- rac{N_h}{N}ight) s_{U,h}^2 ight]. 	ag{3.14} \end{equation}\] Proof. \[ egin{align*} & Var_{SRS}\left(\widehat{T}_{HT}ight) - Var_{strt,prop}\left(\widehat{T}_{HT}ight) \ & =  rac{N}{n}\left(1- rac{n}{N}ight) \left[  rac{N}{N-1}(SSW + SSB) - \left(SSW + \sum_{h=1}^H s_{U,h}^2 ight) ight] \ & =  rac{N}{n}\left(1- rac{n}{N}ight) \left[  rac{N}{N-1}SSB +  rac{1}{N-1}SSW - \sum_{h=1}^H s_{U,h}^2 ight] \ & =  rac{N}{n}\left(1- rac{n}{N}ight) \left[  rac{N}{N-1}SSB +  rac{1}{N-1}SSW - \sum_{h=1}^H s_{U,h}^2 ight] \ & =  rac{N}{n}\left(1- rac{n}{N}ight) \left[  rac{N}{N-1}SSB + \sum_{h=1}^H  rac{N_h-1}{N-1} s_{U,h}^2 - \sum_{h=1}^H s_{U,h}^2 ight] \ & =  rac{N}{n}\left(1- rac{n}{N}ight) \left[  rac{N}{N-1}SSB - \sum_{h=1}^H  rac{N-N_h}{N-1} s_{U,h}^2 ight] \ & =  rac{N}{n}\left(1- rac{n}{N}ight)  rac{N}{N-1} \left[ SSB - \sum_{h=1}^H \left(1- rac{N_h}{N}ight) s_{U,h}^2 ight] \ \end{align*}\] This is greater or equal to 0, that is, the variance from SRS is larger than the variance from stratification under proportional allocation, if \[ SSB \ge \sum_{h=1}^H \left(1- rac{N_h}{N}ight) s_{U,h}^2 \] This happens if \(SSB\) is large and \(s^2_{U,h}\) are small, for \(h = 1, 2, ..., H\). Therefore, to reduce variance using stratification, we should choose a stratification variable so that it is closely related with (i.e., predictive) of the variable of interest \(Y\) so that stratum means \(\overline{y}_{U,h}\) are as different as possible, and unit responses \(y_{h,i}\) are similar within a stratum On another note, we also can prove that if \(N_h\) and \(N\) are large, then variance from stratified sampling under proportional allocation is always smalller than variance from SRS. In other words, we always gain efficiency (i.e., lower variance) with stratification under proportional allocation when strata sizes are large. Proof. When \(N_h\) and \(N\) are large, \[ Ns^2_U =  rac{N}{N-1}SST \approx SST = SSB + SSW \] and \[ \sum_{h=1}^H N_hs_{U,h}^2 = \sum_{h=1}^H rac{N_h}{N_h-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}(y_{h,i}-\overline{y}_{U,h})^2 \approx \sum_{h=1}^H\sum_{i=1}^{N_h}(y_{h,i}-\overline{y}_{U,h})^2 = SSW \] Since \(SSB\) is a sum of squares, it is always positive. Therefore, \(Ns^2_U \ge \sum_{h=1}^H N_hs_{U,h}^2\), and thus \(Var_{SRS}\left(\widehat{T}_{HT}ight) \ge Var_{strt,prop}\left(\widehat{T}_{HT}ight)\) and we always gain efficiency. Example 3.9 Since the number of students in each college is large, we are gaining efficiency when stratifying by college affiliation and do proportional allocation. In contrast, if the stratum sizes are small, stratum means \(\overline{y}_{U,h}\) are similar, and within-stratum variation is large, we will lose efficiency by stratification. In that case, we prefer to use SRS. Example 3.10 Suppose we have two strata: Stratum \(y_{h,i}\) \(\overline{y}_{U,h}\) \(s_{U,h}^2\) \(1\) \(4,9,5\) \( rac{1}{3}(4+9+5) = 6\) \( rac{1}{3-1}\left[(4-6)^2+(9-6)^2+(5-6)^2ight] = 7\) \(2\) \(6,7,8\) \( rac{1}{3}(6+7+8) = 7\) \( rac{1}{3-1}\left[(6-7)^2+(7-7)^2+(8-7)^2ight] = 1\) We can see that, in this case, \(\overline{y}_{U,1}\) is very similar to \(\overline{y}_{U,2}\). At the same time, \(s_{U,1}^2\) is large. We expect that stratification is hurting us. In fact, we have \[ SSB = \sum_{h=1}^H N_h(\overline{y}_{U,h} - \overline{y}_U)^2 = 3	imes (6-6.5)^2 + 3	imes (7-6.5)^2 = 1.5, \] which is smaller than \[ \sum_{h=1}^H\left(1- rac{N_h}{N}ight)s_{U,h}^2 = \left(1- rac{3}{6}ight)	imes 7 + \left(1- rac{3}{6}ight)	imes 1 = 4. \] Exercise 3.1 In Example 3.10, can you re-define two strata, each with 3 units, so that stratification with proportional allocation is better than SRS? 3.3.3 Optimal Allocation 3.3.3.1 Definition As discussed in Section 2.9, in survey sampling, we want to balance two contraries: (i) we want to have a large sample size so that the variance of our estimators are the smallest, on the other hand (ii) we only have limited time and resources to collect the survey information. Optimal allocation is a method in stratified sampling that explicitly minimizes the variance of the HT estimators within a fixed cost constraints. To formalize this mathematically, let \(c_0\) be the known overhead cost of the survey This can be the costs to start the survey such as administrative cost to apply for funding for the survey, to travel to or contact the survey hubs, etc. \(c_h\) be the known cost to sample one unit from stratum \(h\) This can be the cost to print the survey questionnaire, labor costs to interview individuals, etc. Suppose we have a fixed budget amount \(C\), then our unknown (or to-be-determined) sub-sample sizes \(n_1, n_2, ..., n_H\) must satisfy \[ C = c_0 + \sum_{h=1}^H c_hn_h, \] while at the same time minimize10 \[ Var_{strt}(\widehat{T}_{HT}) = \sum_{h=1}^H Var_{SRS}(\widehat{T}_{HT,h}) = \sum_{h=1}^H N_h^2 \left(1- rac{n_h}{N_h}ight) rac{s_{U,h}^2}{n_h}. \] This is a constrained optimization problem where the objective is to minimize the variance subject to the budget constraint. We can prove that the solution \(n_1, ..., n_H\) for this problem satisfy \[ egin{equation} n_h \propto  rac{N_hs_{U,h}}{\sqrt{c_h}} 	ag{3.15} \end{equation}\] Proof. We can solve this constraint optimization by using Lagrange multiplier. In particular, we form the function \[ f(n_1, n_2, ..., n_H, \lambda) = \sum_{h=1}^H N_h^2 \left(1- rac{n_h}{N_h}ight) rac{s_{U,h}^2}{n_h} - \lambda \left(C - c_0 - \sum_{h=1}^H c_hn_hight) \] Take partial derivatives of \(f\) with respect to \(n_h\) and set them to 0 we get \[  rac{\partial f}{\partial n_h} = - rac{N_h^2s_{U,h}^2}{n_h^2} + \lambda c_h = 0 \] This implies \[ n_h =  rac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda}}  rac{N_hs_{U,h}}{\sqrt{c_n}} \propto  rac{N_hs_{U,h}}{\sqrt{c_h}}. \] From Equation (3.15), we can see that in optimal allocation, we will sample more in a stratum \(h\) if \(c_h\) is small, which means it is cheaper to sample in the \(h\)th stratum, and/or \(N_h\) is large, which means the \(h\)th stratum has a lot of units and we need to sample more there to lower our variance, and/or \(s_{U,h}\) is large, which means the responses vary a lot in that stratum and we need to sample more there to lower our variance. Using the same argument as in Equation (3.9), we have \[ egin{equation} n_h = \left( rac{ rac{N_hs_{U,h}}{\sqrt{c_h}}}{\sum_{l=1}^H  rac{N_ls_{U,l}}{\sqrt{c_l}}}ight)n 	ag{3.16} \end{equation}\] 3.3.3.2 Neyman Allocation A special case of optimal allocation is Neyman allocation. When the costs to sample one unit from each stratum are the same, that is, \(c_1 = c_2 = ... = c_H\), then optimal allocation becomes Neyman allocation, where \[ egin{equation} n_h \propto N_hs_{U,h} 	ag{3.17} \end{equation}\] Under stratified sampling with Neyman allocation, the variance of the HT estimator for the population mean is \[ egin{equation} Var_{strt,Ney}(\widehat{T}_{HT}) =  rac{1}{n}\left(\sum_{h=1}^H N_hs_{U,h}ight)^2 - \sum_{h=1}^H N_hs_{U,h}^2. 	ag{3.18} \end{equation}\] Proof. Neyman allocation implies \[ n_h = \left( rac{N_hs_{U,h}}{\sum_{l=1}^H N_ls_{U,l}}ight)n \] Pluggin this into the variance formula for the HT estimator of the population total under stratified sampling, we have \[ egin{align*} Var_{strt}(\widehat{T}_{HT}) & = \sum_{h=1}^H Var_{SRS}(\widehat{T}_{HT,h}) = \sum_{h=1}^H N_h^2 \left(1- rac{n_h}{N_h}ight) rac{s_{U,h}^2}{n_h} \ & = \sum_{h=1}^H N_h^2 rac{s_{U,h}^2}{n_h} - \sum_{h=1}^H N_hs_{U,h}^2 \ & = \sum_{h=1}^H N_h^2  rac{s_{U,h}^2 \sum_{l=1}^H N_ls_{U,l}}{N_hs_{U,h} n} - \sum_{h=1}^H N_hs_{U,h}^2 \ & =  rac{1}{n}\sum_{h=1}^H N_hs_{U,h} \sum_{l=1}^H N_ls_{U,l} - \sum_{h=1}^H N_hs_{U,h}^2 \ & =  rac{1}{n}\left(\sum_{h=1}^H N_hs_{U,h}ight)^2 - \sum_{h=1}^H N_hs_{U,h}^2. \ \end{align*}\] We can prove that, for same sample size \(n\), Neyman allocation is more efficient that proportional allocation, that is \[ egin{equation} Var_{strt,Ney}(\widehat{T}_{HT}) \le Var_{strt,prop}(\widehat{T}_{HT}). 	ag{3.19} \end{equation}\] Proof. Consider \[ egin{align*} & Var_{strt,prop}(\widehat{T}_{HT}) - Var_{strt,Ney}(\widehat{T}_{HT}) \ & \qquad =  rac{N}{n}\left(1- rac{n}{N}ight)\sum_{h=1}^HN_hs_{U,h}^2 - \left( rac{1}{n}\left(\sum_{h=1}^H N_hs_{U,h}ight)^2 - \sum_{h=1}^H N_hs_{U,h}^2ight) \ & \qquad =  rac{N}{n}\sum_{h=1}^HN_hs_{U,h}^2 - \sum_{h=1}^HN_hs_{U,h}^2 -  rac{1}{n}\left(\sum_{h=1}^H N_hs_{U,h}ight)^2 + \sum_{h=1}^H N_hs_{U,h}^2 \ & \qquad =  rac{N}{n}\sum_{h=1}^HN_hs_{U,h}^2 -  rac{1}{n}\left(\sum_{h=1}^H N_hs_{U,h}ight)^2 \ & \qquad =  rac{N^2}{n}\left[\sum_{h=1}^H rac{N_h}{N}s_{U,h}^2 - \left(\sum_{h=1}^H  rac{N_h}{N}s_{U,h}ight)^2ight] \ & \qquad =  rac{N^2}{n}\sum_{h=1}^H rac{N_h}{N}(s_{U,h} -  ar{s})^2 \ge 0, \end{align*}\] where \( ar{s} = \sum_{h=1}^N rac{N_h}{N}s_{U,h}\). 3.3.3.3 Comparing to Proportional Allocation Recall that Proportional allocation: \(n_h \propto N_h\) Optimal allocation: \(n_h \propto  rac{N_hs_{U,h}}{\sqrt{c}_h}\) So optimal allocation and proportional allocation are equivalent when \(s_{U,1} = s_{U,2} = ... = s_{U,H}\) and \(c_{U,1} = c_{U,2} = ... = c_{U,H}\). Note that although it is better than proportional allocation since it is the optimizer, optimal allocation only works for one variable of interest because: the optimal allocation \(n_h\), as in Equation (3.16), depends on \(s_{U,h}\), which in turn depends on \(y_{h,i}\), the one specific variable of interest. Often, in survey sampling, we have more than one variable of interest. For example, not only we are interested in students’ rent payment, but we may also be interested in other types of expense as well. If \(s_{U,h}\) and \(c_{U,h}\) are almost the similar among clusters, the gain in doing optimal allocation is small and we better use proportional allocation. Example 3.11 Suppose we want to sample \(n = 36\) students for a survey about test scores. To design this year’s survey, we consider two strata of sizes 1200 and 800, respectively, and we take the means and standard deviations from last year’s survey and get Stratum \(N_h\) \(\overline{y}_{U,h}\) \(s_{U,h}\) \(N_h s_{U,h}\) \(N_h s_{U,h}^2\) \(1\) \(1,200\) \(60\) \(15\) \(18,000\) \(270,000\) \(2\) \(800\) \(80\) \(10\) \(8,000\) \(80,000\) Total \(2,000\) \(26,000\) \(350,000\) If we use stratified sampling, proportional allocation: Since \(n_h =  rac{n}{N}N_h\), \(n_1 =  rac{36}{2000}	imes 1200 = 21.6 \approx 22\) and \(n_2 =  rac{36}{2000} 	imes 800 = 14.4 \approx 14\). \(Var_{strt, prop}(\widehat{T}_{HT}) =  rac{N}{n}\left(1- rac{n}{N}ight)\sum_{h=1}^HN_hs_{U,h}^2 =  rac{2000}{36}\left(1 -  rac{36}{2000}ight)350,000=19,094,444\). \(Var_{strt, prop}(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT}) =  rac{1}{N^2}Var_{strt, prop}(\widehat{T}_{HT}) = 4.7736\) If we use stratified sampling, Neyman allocation: Since \(n_h = \left( rac{N_hs_{U,h}}{\sum_{l=1}^H N_ls_{U,l}}ight)n\), \(n_1 =  rac{18,000}{26,000}	imes 36 = 24.9231 \approx 25\) and \(n_2 =  rac{8,000}{26,000} 	imes 36 = 11.0769 \approx 11\). \(Var_{strt, Ney}(\widehat{T}_{HT}) =  rac{1}{n}\left(\sum_{h=1}^H N_hs_{U,h}ight)^2 - \sum_{h=1}^H N_hs_{U,h}^2\) \(=  rac{1}{36}	imes 26,000^2 - 350,000 = 18,427,778\) \(Var_{strt, Ney}(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT}) =  rac{1}{N^2}Var_{strt, Ney}(\widehat{T}_{HT}) = 4.6069\) If we use SRS, \(Var_{SRS}(\widehat{T}_{HT}) = N^2\left(1- rac{n}{N}ight) rac{s_U^2}{n} = N^2\left(1- rac{n}{N}ight) rac{(SSB+SSW)}{n(N-1)}\) \(SSB = \sum_{h=1}^H N_h(\overline{y}_{h,U} - \overline{y}_U)^2 = 1,200 	imes (60-68)^2 + 800 	imes (80-68)^2 = 192,000\). \(SSW = \sum_{h=1}^H (N_h - 1)s_h^2 = 350,000 - 15^2 - 10^2 = 349,675\) \(Var_{SRS}(\widehat{T}_{HT}) = 2000^2	imes \left(1 -  rac{36}{2000}ight) rac{192,000 + 349,675}{36	imes 1999} = 29,566,164\). \(Var_{SRS}(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT}) =  rac{1}{N^2}Var_{SRS}(\widehat{T}_{HT}) = 7.3915\). 3.3.4 Design Effect The design effect of a complex survey is defined as \[ DE =  rac{Var_{complex}(\widehat{	heta}_n)}{Var_{SRS}(\widehat{	heta}_n)}, \] in which the estimators \(\hat{	heta}\) are calculated based on a sample size of \(n\) observation units. The interpretation of design effect is that we gain/lose \((1 - DE)	imes 100\%\) of efficiency when using the complex sampling design instead of SRS. Furthermore, we need \( rac{n}{DE}\) observations under SRS to achieve the same variance as in the complex sampling scheme with \(n\) observations. We call \(n_{eff} =  rac{n}{DE}\) the effective sample size. Example 3.12 In Example 3.11, the design effect of stratified sampling with proportional allocation is \[  rac{Var_{strt, prop}(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT})}{Var_{SRS}(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT})} =  rac{4.7736}{7.3915} =0.6458 \] This means that we need \(36/0.65 = 23.4 \approx 55\) observations in SRS to achieve the same variance as in the stratified sample with proportional allocation with \(36\) observations. The effective sample size here is \(55\). 3.3.5 Sample Size Determination Similar to Section 2.9, for proportional allocation, \[ Var_{strt, prop}(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT}) =  rac{1}{n}\left(1- rac{n}{N}ight)\sum_{h=1}^H rac{N_h}{N}s_{U,h}^2 \] so setting the tolerable margin of error \(e = z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{Var_{strt, prop}(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT})}\), we can calculate the sample size to be \[ n_{prop} =  rac{\sum_{h=1}^H rac{N_h}{N}s_{U,h}^2}{ rac{e^2}{z_{\alpha/2}^2} +  rac{1}{N}\sum_{h=1}^H rac{N_h}{N}s_{U,h}^2} \] For Neyman allocation, since \[ Var_{strt, Ney}(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT}) =  rac{1}{n}\left(\sum_{h=1}^H  rac{N_h}{N}s_{U,h}ight)^2 -  rac{1}{N}\sum_{h=1}^H  rac{N_h}{N}s_{U,h}^2. \] so setting the tolerable margin of error \(e = z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{Var_{strt, Ney}(\widehat{\overline{Y}}_{HT})}\), we can calculate the sample size to be \[ n_{Ney} =  rac{\left(\sum_{h=1}^H rac{N_h}{N}s_{U,h}ight)^2}{ rac{e^2}{z_{\alpha/2}^2} +  rac{1}{N}\sum_{h=1}^H rac{N_h}{N}s_{U,h}^2}. \] This is similar to partitioning in mathematics.↩︎ We consider the variance of the HT estimator for the total here because variance of the HT estimator for the mean is just the variance of the HT estimator for the total divided by \(N^2\), so minimizing the variance of the HT estimator for the mean is the same as minimizing the variance of HT estimator for the total.↩︎
